coredata.dc:title: Aggregation enhanced emissive orange carbon dots for information encryption and detection of Fe3+ and tetracycline
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Chunyan
Liu, Lei
Zhang, Daohan
coredata.dc:description: In this study, N-doped fluorescent carbon dots with aggregation enhanced emission (N-CDs) were synthesized by a simple and rapid microwave-assisted method using o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and urea as raw materials and water as solvent. The fluorescence quantum yield of N-CDs was 20.64 %. N-CDs can be applied as invisible inks for message encryption. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs can be quenched by Fe3+ and enhanced by tetracycline (TC). Therefore, two fluorescent probes were simultaneously designed in this study. Namely, “turn-off” fluorescence probe for Fe3+ and “turn-on” fluorescence probe for TC. The linear detection range of Fe3+ is from 1 to 70 μM, and detection limit is 0.1011 μM; the linear detection range of TC is from 0.1 to 10 μM, and the detection limit can be as low as 0.0555 μM. In this paper, the mutual interference between Fe3+ and TC was investigated for the first time. The detection of Fe3+ and TC was made more accurate by optimizing pH conditions and adding masking agent.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Aggregate enhanced luminescence
Invisible Ink
Fe3+
Tetracycline

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: AIE properties and application in information encryption
Mutual interference study of Fe3+ and TC
AIE properties and application in information encryption
Mutual interference of Fe3+ and TC

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21102032 ), Key Project of Biomedicine Joint Fund of Hebei Natural Science Foundation ( B2020208043 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM of N-CDs (inset at the bottom right: particle size distribution of N-CDs); (b) UV, excitation, and emission spectra of N-CDs in DMF solution (inset: photos of N-CDs under daylight and 365 nm UV lamp); (c) fluorescence spectra of N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths; (d) FT-IR spectra of N-CDs.
(a) XPS spectra of N-CDs; (b) High resolution XPS spectrum of C1s; (c) O1s; (d) N1s.

(a) Photographs of solid N-CDs under daylight and 365 nm UV lamps; (b) Fluorescence profiles of N-CDs with different volume share of water in the solvent system (inset: corresponding photographs under 365 nm UV lamps); (c)(d) The filter paper after writing with N-CDs solution under daylight and 365 nm UV lamp.

(a) Fluorescence quenching of different cations (10-3M) on N-CDs at 419 nm excitation wavelength; (b) Fluorescence quenching of different cations (10-3M) on N-CDs and other cations effect on Fe3+detection; (c) Photos of N-CDs after the addition of different metal ions (10-3M) under daylight and 365 nm UV lamps; (d) Photographs of N-CDs mixed with Fe3+ and Cu2+ at concentrations of 10-4M and 10-5M under daylight.

(a) Fluorescence intensity profiles of N-CDs solutions at Fe3+ concentrations of 0, 1, 3, 4, 6, 8, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70 μM at 419 nm excitation wavelength; (b) linear plots of (I0-I)/I0 versus Fe3+ concentration in the concentration range of 1 ∼ 70 μM; (c) UV absorption profiles of solutions of N-CDs, N-CDs-Fe3+ under concentrations of 100 μM and10 μM, respectively; (d) linear plots of Fe3+ concentration versus I0/I at different temperatures (5 °C, 25 °C, 70 °C); (e) Transient fluorescence spectra of N-CDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+.

(a) Fluorescence intensity of N-CDs with different antibiotics (10-4M) at excitation wave-length 365 nm; (b) Fluorescence intensity of N-CDs + antibiotics (10-4M) and other antibiotics on the effect of TC detection; (c) N-CDs under daylight and 365 nm UV lamp after the addition of different kinds of antibiotics (10-4M).

(a) fluorescence spectra of N-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of TC (0 ∼ 20 μM); (b) plot of linear relationship between TC concentration and (I-I0)/I0 in the range of 0.1 ∼ 10 μM; (c) UV absorption profiles of N-CDs and after the addition of TC (5 μM).

(a) Fluorescence of the detection of Fe3+(50 μM) at pH 4 in the presence of TC (5 μM) (Top right inset: histogram of fluorescence intensity); (b) Fluorescence of detection of TC (5 μM) at pH 5 in the presence of Fe3+(50 μM) (Top right inset: histogram of fluorescence intensity).
Comparison of detection limits for Fe3+ using fluorescence method.
Comparision of different methods for the determination of TC.

head.ce:title: Aggregation enhanced emissive orange carbon dots for information encryption and detection of Fe3+ and tetracycline

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Engineering
Hebei University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, N-doped fluorescent carbon dots with aggregation enhanced emission (N-CDs) were synthesized by a simple and rapid microwave-assisted method using o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and urea as raw materials and water as solvent. The fluorescence quantum yield of N-CDs was 20.64 %. N-CDs can be applied as invisible inks for message encryption. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs can be quenched by Fe3+ and enhanced by tetracycline (TC). Therefore, two fluorescent probes were simultaneously designed in this study. Namely, “turn-off” fluorescence probe for Fe3+ and “turn-on” fluorescence probe for TC. The linear detection range of Fe3+ is from 1 to 70 μM, and detection limit is 0.1011 μM; the linear detection range of TC is from 0.1 to 10 μM, and the detection limit can be as low as 0.0555 μM. In this paper, the mutual interference between Fe3+ and TC was investigated for the first time. The detection of Fe3+ and TC was made more accurate by optimizing pH conditions and adding masking agent.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots, as a novel nanomaterial, have received much attention for their unique optical properties, high photostability, non-toxicity and excellent biocompatibility [1–3]. With these unique advantages, CDs have become very popular in many areas, such as chemical sensing [4], bioimaging [5], drug carriers [6], catalysis [7], and enhanced fingerprint recognition [8]. There are many methods for synthesizing carbon dots, mainly divided into “top-down” and “bottom-up” synthesis methods, according to the size of the raw material [9–11]. Microwave-assisted method among the “bottom-up” methods is currently recognized as a better synthesis method. Compared with the commonly used solvent/hydrothermal synthesis method, it is not only easy to operate, but also greatly reduces the synthesis time [12].

As an essential element in the human body, Fe3+ plays an important role in many physiological activities, including enzyme catalysis, oxygen transport, electron transfer and hemoglobin synthesis [13,14]. Deficiency of Fe3+ can cause many diseases, such as iron deficiency anemia. On the other hand, Fe3+ as a heavy metal ion will lead to water pollution and do serious harm to the human health. For example, excessive Fe3+ will cause liver damage, kidney failure, heart disease and cancer, etc. With the development of industry, many enterprises are discharging wastewater into rivers, leading to serious water pollution. Therefore, accurate detection of Fe3+ content is crucial. Some literatures have been published on the use of carbon dots as fluorescent probes to detect Fe3+ [15–17]. Yu et al. synthesized an N-doped carbon dot for Fe3+ detection by microwave method using glycine as raw material with linear detection range of 12.5 μM ∼ 250 μM [18]. Raji et al. synthesized an N-doped carbon dot by hydrothermal method using triglycerides and ammonia to detect Fe3+ with a detection limit of 0.9 μM [19]. Further studies targeting efficient synthesis and lower detection limit are in route.

Tetracycline (TC) is a member of the tetracycline (TCs) antibiotics and has antibacterial activity against a wide range of Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, atypical organisms. Because of its broad antibacterial activity, TC was previously widely used for the treatment of infectious diseases in humans and animals. Adverse effects such as tetracycline teeth, allergic reactions, gastrointestinal disturbances and hepatotoxicity can occur when people use too much TC [20,21]. Due to its side effects, it is currently used less in the treatment of human diseases, but is still widely used in animal husbandry to promote animal growth and treat infectious diseases [22], which leads to a large amount of TC residues in livestock products. Long-term ingestion of food containing TC residues can lead to pathogen resistance to antibiotics, which poses a serious threat to human health [23], therefore, the accurate determination of TC content appears to be crucial. Several analytical methods have been applied for the determination of tetracycline content, such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [24–26] high performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC-MSIMS) [27,28], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [29,30], electrochemical method (ECR) [31–33] and immunoassay (IA) [34,35], etc. These methods have drawbacks such as complicated operation process, time-consuming procedures, and expensive instruments or raw materials required. Hence, it is necessary to develop an efficient, economical and simple method for detecting TC in practical applications. Fluorescence analysis has the advantages of simpler operation and more sensitive response, which provides a better choice for TC detection. Although the use of carbon dots for TC detection has been reported in the literature, e.g., Yan et al. synthesized yellow-green fluorescent carbon dots based on the principle of amine-formaldehyde condensation using o-phenylenediamine and glyoxal, and TC was detected by quenching the fluorescence of the carbon dots [36]. Wu et al. achieved selective detection of TC by fluorescence enhancement by a hybrid fluorescent probes composed of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) and Eu3+ (N-CDs-Eu3+) [37], but they suffered from a series of drawbacks, such as complicated operation and long reaction time.

In this study, N-doped carbon dots with enhanced emission property (N-CDs) is synthesized through an efficient microwave-assisted method. Based on its aggregation-induced emission (AIE) properties, its application in information encryption was investigated, and it was successfully used as an invisible ink. The obtained products N-CDs are selective for Fe3+ and TC. Fe3+ quenches the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs and TC enhances the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs, which indicates that a dual probe is designed to detect Fe3+ and TC simultaneously. Meanwhile, in this study, we proposed for the first time how carbon dots can exclude the mutual interference between Fe3+ and TC when they are detected at the same time.

All purchased reagents and chemicals were used directly without further purification. The experimental water was deionized water. o-phenylenediamine (OPD) (Macklin), urea (Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory), DMF (Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory), copper nitrate (Macklin), zinc chloride (Macklin), magnesium chloride anhydrous (Macklin), chromium nitrate nonahydrate (Macklin), potassium chloride (Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory), cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Macklin), ferrous sulfate (Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory), aluminum chloride anhydrous (Macklin), ferric nitrate nonahydrate (Macklin), cuprous chloride (Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory), anhydrous calcium chloride (Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory), sodium chloride (Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory), tetracycline (Macklin), ornidazole (Macklin), metronidazole (Macklin), chloramphenicol (Macklin), ofloxacin (Macklin), levofloxacin (Macklin), norfloxacin (Macklin), chlortetracycline(Macklin), oxytetracycline(Macklin), triethanolamine (Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory), Rhodamine B (Macklin).

The N-CDs were synthesized using an APL microwave digestion instrument (APL Technology Group Co., Ltd.), dried in a DZ-28C vacuum drying oven (Tianjin Teste Instrument Co., Ltd.). The fluorescence spectrum was measured by a F97-Pro fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shanghai Prism Technology Co., Ltd.). The UV–visible spectrum was measured by a TU-1900 double-beam UV spectrophotometer (UV-Beijing Pu-analysis General Instrument Ltd.). The compositions of N-CDs were characterized by JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan), Nicolet 6700 infrared Raman spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA), and ESCALAB 250Xi x-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS. Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA).

The OPD (1.0915 g) and urea (0.6080 g) were sonicated for 5 min to in 40 mL of water, then transferred to a 100 mL reaction kettle, heated at 180 °C for 30 min and cooled naturally to room temperature. A mixture of orange-yellow solid and tan liquid was obtained, and the solid product was collected by filtering through filter paper. Then the solid product was dissolved in DMF to obtain a yellow carbon dots solution for detection experiments. The stability of the carbon dots was examined by varying the time and temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: AIE properties and application in information encryption
ce:section.ce:para: Water was chosen as the poor solvent and DMF as good solvent to study the AIE properties of N-CDs. The volume proportion of water was varied (10 %, 30 %, 50 %, 70 %, 90 %), and the color of N-CDs dissolved in the different solvent systems was photographed under the 365 nm UV lamp. The relationship between N-CDs concentration and fluorescence intensity was investigated by dissolving 0.01 mg to 0.3 mg of N-CDs in 10 mL of DMF, and then testing their fluorescence spectra. The corresponding fluorescence profiles were measured. The N-CDs solution was used as ink and written on the filter paper with a pen. After the filter paper was completely dried, its photographs were recorded in daylight and under a 365 nm UV lamp.

Eleven cations (Cu2+, Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, K+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Fe3+) were selected in sensoring experiment. At room temperature, 0.5 mL of cations were mixed with 4.5 mL of N-CDs solution, respectively, the fluorescence spectra of the mixed solutions were measured, and the corresponding fluorescence intensities were recorded. Interference experiments were also performed to verify that the detection of Fe3+ would not be interfered with other cations.

To ensure more accurate and sensitive detection of Fe3+, different temperature, time and pH gradients were set to screen the optimal detection conditions. In order to investigate the detection limit of N-CDs on Fe3+, 4.5 mL of N-CDs solution was mixed with 0.5 mL of different concentrations of Fe3+ under optimal detection conditions. The excitation wavelength was set to 419 nm, and the fluorescence intensity was recorded by a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The detection limit was calculated by LOD = 3σ/K, where “σ” is the standard deviation of blank samples and “K” is the slope of the standard curve.

To test whether the synthesized N-CDs could selectively detect antibiotics, eight common antibiotics were selected (Metronidazole (MTR), Ornidazole (ORN), Chloramphenicol (CHL), Ofloxacin (OFL), Levofloxacin (LEV), Norfloxacin (NOR), Chlortetracycline (CTC), oxytetracycline (OTC)). We also screened the assay conditions. Under optimal detection conditions, the selectivity and detection limit experiments were performed in the same way as for Fe3+.

Tap water was used as the actual sample to detect the Fe3+ content. 4.5 mL of N-CDs solution was mixed with 0.5 mL of tap water containing different concentrations of Fe3+, and the fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution was measured. Milk samples were purchased from local supermarkets. The milk samples were first processed according to the literature method: 10 mL of milk was mixed with 20 mL of chloroform and 20 mL of trichloroacetic acid (20 % (w/v)), and the mixture was ultrasonicated for 10 min to precipitate proteins and fats, and then centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 20 min to collect the supernatant [5]. 4.5 mL of N-CDs and 0.5 mL of milk supernatant (4 μM, 6 μM, and 8 μM of TC were added to the milk supernatant, respectively) were mixed, and the fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution was measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mutual interference study of Fe3+ and TC
ce:section.ce:para: In order to eliminate the interference of TC against Fe3+ detection, we set the assay condition to pH 4. Under this condition, we measured the fluorescence spectra of N-CDs, N-CDs + Fe3+ (50 μM), N-CDs + Fe3+ (50 μM) + TC (5 μM).

In order to eliminate the interference of Fe3+ (50 μM) on TC (5 μM) detection, triethanolamine (TEA) was chosen as the masking agent for Fe3+ in this study. The solution with TC present in the N-CD was used as a blank solution. The fluorescence spectra of N-CD + TC, N-CD + TC + Fe3+ (50 μM), N-CDs + Fe3+ (50 μM) + TEA (0.05 g/mL) were tested. Compare the fluorescence spectra of the three solutions.

In order to characterize the morphology and size distribution of N-CDs, TEM was performed and the results showed that N-CDs are uniformly dispersed. Spherical nanoparticles with an average diameter of 7 nm (110 N-CDs were measured) and distinct lattice stripes can be observed and lattice spacing is 0.24 nm (Fig. 1 a). The UV and fluorescence of N-CDs were characterized, as shown in Fig. 1b. The optimal excitation wavelength of N-CDs in DMF solution was 419 nm and the corresponding emission wavelength was 524 nm. The solution is light yellow transparent under natural light, and green fluorescence is observed under 365 nm UV lamp. In Fig. 1c, it can be seen that the emission peak of N-CDs does not change with varied excitation wavelength, which indicates its excitation wavelength independence characteristics, attributed to its uniform size distribution [38]. FT-IR spectroscopy and XPS were used to analyze the functional groups on the surface of N-CDs and elemental composition. The FT-IR results (Fig. 1d) demonstrate that the N-CDs have –NH2, –OH and C = O and C = C on the surface. The broad band around 3126 cm−1 and 3028 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of O–H and N–H bonds. The broad peak at 2808.8 cm−1 was attributed to C–H stretching vibration. The peak at 1742 cm−1 originated from the stretching vibration of C = O bond. The peak at 1629.3 cm−1 was attributed to C = C stretching vibration. The peak at 1197.6 cm−1 was attributed to C-N bending vibration. The surface elemental states of N-CDs were investigated using XPS, and the results are shown in Fig. 2 a. Three different peaks centered at 284.6, 400.2 and 531.6 eV were attributed to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively, with 69.74 % of C1s, 21.13 % of N1s and 9.13 % of O1s. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 2b) shows three peaks at 284.6, 285.4 and 288.8 eV, attributed to C = C, C-N and C = O, respectively; the peak at 531.6 eV in the O1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) is attributed to C = O; the N1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) shows two characteristic peaks near 398.6 and 400.2 eV, confirming the presence of N–H and C-N, consistent with FT-IR characterization results. The results of the stability experiments (Fig. S1a, b) show that the fluorescence intensity of synthesized N-CDs remains basically unchanged with the change of time and temperature, which indicates that its fluorescence properties are stable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: AIE properties and application in information encryption
ce:section.ce:para: Because the solid N-CDs had bright orange fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp (as shown in Fig. 3 a), we speculate that it may have AIE properties. To prove this conjecture, water was chosen as a poor solvent and DMF as good solvent to investigate the fluorescence of N-CDs in mixed solvents [39]. The dissolving property of N-CDs in water and DMF is revealed in Fig. S2a which shows that N-CDs are insoluble in water and soluble in DMF. The fluorescence profiles of the solutions were measured at different water volume proportions (10 %, 30 %, 50 %, 70 %, and 90 %). It can be seen from Fig. 3b that the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs gradually increased and the emission wavelength showed an obvious red shift with the increase of the water volume proportions. The inset in the upper left corner of Fig. 3b shows the color of N-CDs under 365 nm UV lamp, which can be visualized that the solution fluorescence color changes from green fluorescence to orange fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp as the percentage of water volume increases. Noticeably, the solution with 90 % water proportion shows orange fluorescence similar to solid N-CDs. The relationship between the concentration of N-CDs and the fluorescence intensity in Fig. S2b shows that the fluorescence intensity is stronger when the concentration of N-CDs is higher, which further proves its AIE effect. Based on the observations, we conclude that N-CDs have AIE properties. We apply N-CDs as invisible inks for information encryption. As seen in Fig. 3c, d, the text on the filter paper is unreadable under daylight, while shows a bright yellow-green fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp. This indicates that our carbon dots can be applied as invisible ink for information encryption.

The FT-IR results demonstrate that our N-CDs have –NH2, –COOH, and other groups on the surface, which endow N-CDs detection ability. To study the detection of synthesized CDs for cations, the fluorescence of N-CDs with eleven cations were measured. As shown in Fig. 4 a, b, only Fe3+ (10-3M) greatly decrease the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs and cause red shift of the emission and the presence of other cations does not interfere with the detection of Fe3+, which indicates that N-CDs have excellent selectivity for Fe3+. This result is also supported by the fluorescence photographs of their solutions under 365 nm UV lamp. It is obvious from Fig. 4c, that the N-CDs showed obvious discoloration of the solution under both daylight and 365 nm UV lamp after the addition of Fe3+ (10-3M). The color of N-CDs was also altered under daylight after the addition of Cu2+ (10-4M). Namely, Cu2+ and Fe3+ can be directly identified by the naked eye at concentrations of 10-3M and 10-4M. However, further reduction of the concentration does not enable naked eye identification (Fig. 4d).

Due to the fluorescence quenching effect of Fe3+ to N-CDs, its detection limit was studied further. In order to acquire reliable and accurate detection, we firstly optimized the detection conditions. We can see from Fig. S3 that the response time is within 1 min. The optimal detection temperature is 5–25 °C, and the pH range is 1–13. Under the optimal testing conditions. As shown in Fig. 5 a, b, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs gradually decreased with the addition of Fe3+ (0 ∼ 70 μM), showing a typical concentration-dependent behavior. By fitting the above linear relationship, the equation for the quantitative detection of Fe3+ concentration could be obtained:

(I0-I)/I0 = 0.0028C + 0.0003, R2 = 0.9967 (where “I0” represent the fluorescence intensity of blank N-CDs, “I” represent the fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution of N-CDs and Fe3+, and “C” represent the concentration of Fe3+). The detection limit was calculated according to the formula LOD = 3σ/k. LOD = 0.1011 μM. The obtained detection limit was much lower than the maximum content of Fe3+ in drinking water (<5.35 μM) in the National Drinking Water Quality Standards of China and the United States, so this N-CDs is expected to be applied to the detection of Fe3+ in drinking water. Table 1. summarizes detection results of Fe3+ in some literatures. We can see that our N-CDs is advantageous in terms of detection limits.

In general, fluorescence quenching process refers to the interaction between the fluorescent molecules and the quencher molecules, which usually originates from dynamic or static quenching. It is known that Fe3+ reacts with groups of –OH, –COOH or –NH2 distributed on the surface of CDs, which leads to the generation of new non-radiative chelates or coordination compounds, triggering fluorescence quenching [43,44]. In order to investigate the fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-CDs by Fe3+ under different concentrations, the UV absorption profiles of N-CDs were measured in the presence of two Fe3+ concentrations at 100 μM and 10 μM. The results in Fig. 5c show that when 100 μM Fe3+ was added, the shape of its UV absorption spectrum changed, the absorption peak was red-shifted. At the Fe3+ concentration of 10 μM, the position and shape of the UV absorption peak were basically unchanged. This phenomenon suggests that the fluorescence quenching caused by Fe3+ in this study is divided into two types, static quenching when the Fe3+ concentration is 100 μM and dynamic quenching when the Fe3+ concentration is 10 μM. We can speculate that the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs by Fe3+ in the linear detection range (0 ∼ 70 μM) is dynamic quenching, and the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs by Fe3+ under concentrations above 70 μM is static quenching. The results of the variable temperature experiments in Fig. 5d help us verify that the fluorescence quenching at Fe3+ concentrations in the linear range (0 ∼ 70 μM) belongs to dynamic quenching. The dynamic quenching is due to the collision between the Fe3+ and the N-CDs resulting in a decrease in fluorescence intensity. At higher temperature, the turbulence of the solution system increases then the collision chances increase, which leads to an increase in the quenching constant, i.e., the slope at high temperature is greater than that at low temperature (Magnitude of slope at different temperatures: 0.041 (70℃) ＞0.028 (25℃) ＞0.024 (5℃)). Therefore, the fluorescence quenching caused by Fe3+ in the linear detection range (0 ∼ 70 μM) is dominated by dynamic quenching. The fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs without or with Fe3+ ions are given in Fig. 5e. It can be found that the fluorescence lifetime of the N-CDs was significantly reduced from 3.97 ns to 3.72 ns upon the addition of 10 μM Fe3+, which further proves that the fluorescence quenching of the N-CDs by Fe3+ is dynamic quenching process. For solutions with concentrations above 70 μM, the quenching mechanism changed to static quenching, so the linear relationship is no longer followed at high concentrations.

The property of N-CDs on the detection of TC was investigated, as shown in Fig. 6 a, b, the presence of TC (10-4M) caused a significant fluorescence increase of the N-CDs (at the excitation wavelength setting of 365 nm), while its fluorescence intensity did not change obviously in the presence of other antibiotics at the same concentration. Meanwhile, the results in Fig. 6b show that the presence of other antibiotics had no significant effect on the detection of TC. The above experimental results indicate that N-CDs have good selectivity for TC.

The results of Fig. S4b, c show that the optimal detection temperature for detecting TC is 5 ∼ 25 °C and the pH condition is 5 ∼ 7. Under the optimal testing conditions, we further investigated the detection limit of N-CDs for TC. In the range of TC concentration 0 ∼ 20 μM, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs gradually increased with the increase of TC concentration (Fig. 7 a). As shown in Fig. 7b, (I0-I)/I0 showed a good linear correlation with TC concentration in the range of 0.1 ∼ 10 μM. “I0” was the fluorescence intensity value of the blank sample (without TC addition), and “I” was the fluorescence intensity value after adding different concentrations of TC. The concentration of TC was expressed as C (μM). The linear regression equation was (I0-I)/I0 = 0.1135C + 0.085 with the correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9954. The detection limit of TC was obtained as 0.0555 μM according to the equation of LOD = 3σ/k. Compared to other literatures about TC detection, N-CDs exhibits higher sensitivity. At the same time, our detection method has a very promising application in comparison to other detection methods due to low cost and effective nature. Table 2 summarizes the detection limits and linearity towards TC in literatures.

In most cases, TC achieves being detected by quenching the fluorescence of carbon dots [45], while an notable phenomenon is observed in our study: the addition of TC resulted in a significant enhancement of the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs. According to the literature antibiotics enhance the fluorescence of carbon dots mainly due to three reasons: the first one is the effect of electrostatic repulsion, when antibiotics adsorb on the surface of carbon dots, it increases the distance between carbon dots, thus reducing the non-radiative leap leading to fluorescence enhancement; the second reason is that antibiotics act as a ligand that can eliminate the surface defects of carbon dots and eventually enhance the fluorescence of carbon dots [46]; the third one is caused by aggregation-induced emission (AIE) [47], where antibiotics can cause the aggregation of CDs thus enhancing its fluorescence. A conjecture was made to explain the fluorescence enhancement phenomenon in this paper: the coexistence of chromophore and auxochrome (e.g.: C = O, –OH, –NH2) leads to enhanced fluorescence. The results of the UV spectrum showed that the UV absorption peak of TC broadened and red-shifted after mixing with the carbon dot solution (Fig. 7c), indicating that TC bind with N-CDs. FT-IR results showed that N-CDs surface did contain a large amount of –NH2 and C = O, so the –NH2 and –OH on the surface of TC might combine with –NH2 and –OH groups on the carbon dot surface through hydrogen bonding interactions leading to TC adsorption on the carbon dots, resulting in the enhancement of the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots. In addition, the attachment of TC may alter the surface defects of the carbon dots, which leads to enhanced fluorescence intensity. However, the exact mechanism of fluorescence enhancement needs to be further investigated.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of N-CDs was determined in reference to rhodamine B. The fluorescence quantum yield of N-CDs was calculated as 20.64 % according to the following formula:
Y u
=
Y s
×
F u
F s
×
A s
A u
×
φ u
φ s
× 100 %

In order to verify the feasibility of N-CDs in detecting real samples, N-CDs were used to determine the Fe3+ content in tap water as well as the TC content in the milk samples. The results of Fe3+ detection are shown in Table 3 . The average recoveries ranged from 99.25 % to 101.5 % with the associated standard deviation below 3.35 %. The results of TC assay are shown in Table 4 , where the average recoveries ranged from 94.25 % to 99.00 % and the associated standard deviations did not exceed 5.47 %. The results of real samples showed that the proposed method for the determination of Fe3+ and TC in this study is reliable and can be used for the detection of real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mutual interference of Fe3+ and TC
ce:section.ce:para: Although Fe3+ and TC are detected in different ways, sometimes Fe3+ often coexists with TC in real samples. However, in the reported literatures on detecting either Fe3+ or TC, researchers often overlook their mutual interference. In this study, we for the first time investigate the mutual interference between Fe3+ and TC and seek practical solutions to eliminate their interference against each other. From the results of Fig. 8 a we can clearly see that the presence of TC does not affect the results of Fe3+ detection by N-CDs when solution pH is 4. We speculate that this may be related to the nature of TC, which is unstable under acidic conditions and produces an inactive orange-yellow dehydrated substance. Therefore, in pH 4 solution TC could not induce fluorescence enhancement to N-CDs and would not cause interfere to the detection of Fe3+. We chose triethanolamine (TEA) as the masking agent for Fe3+ to eliminate the interference of Fe3+ on TC. TEA is a common chelating agent which is used as masking agent of Fe3+ in our study. The results in Fig. 8b demonstrate that the addition of TEA can indeed eliminate the interference of Fe3+ on TC detection well at pH 5.

In summary, we synthesized N-CDs by microwave-assisted method using OPD and urea as raw materials and water as solvent. In addition to applying N-CDs to invisible ink, we designed it as a dual fluorescent probe. N-CDs showed “turn-off” detection to Fe3+ and “turn-on” detection to TC. The linear detection range of Fe3+ was 1 ∼ 70 μM with the detection limit of 0.1011 μM, and the linear detection range of TC was 0.1 ∼ 10 μM with the detection limit as low as 0.0555 μM, much lower than the allowance for TC residue standards in meat products and water (<100 μg/kg) stipulated by China and international organizations such as the EU. N-CDs were successfully applied to the detection in real samples with satisfactory results. For the first time, we investigated the mutual interference between Fe3+ and TC and seek solutions to eliminate the interference. Under optimized pH conditions and adding a masking agent, Fe3+ and TC can be detected accurately. This provides a good idea for the future detection by fluorescence sensors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21102032), Key Project of Biomedicine Joint Fund of Hebei Natural Science Foundation (B2020208043).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123504.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
